elements.text: Additional Financial Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae may, at any time based on its view of a seller/servicer's financial strength or its assessment of market conditions or other relevant factors, impose additional financial requirements, including enhanced net worth, capital, or liquidity requirements, as well as provisions related to the items in the Additional Financial Requirements table below.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Any additional requirements Fannie Mae imposes may apply to a particular seller/servicer, a defined group or type of seller/servicer, or all sellers/servicers. A seller/servicer's failure to comply with any additional requirements may result in Fannie Mae declaring a breach of the Lender Contract.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae may declare a breach of the Lender Contract if any of the circumstances described in the following table occur.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Category
elements.label: text

elements.text: Description
elements.label: text

elements.text: Material Decline in Lender Adjusted
elements.label: text

elements.text: Net Worth
elements.label: text

elements.text: Typically, a decline is material if Lender Adjusted Net Worth declines by more than 25% over a
elements.label: text

elements.text: quarterly reporting period or by more than 40% over two-consecutive quarterly reporting periods.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Decline in Profitability
elements.label: text

elements.text: Four or more consecutive quarterly losses accompanied by a decline in Lender Adjusted Net Worth of
elements.label: text

elements.text: 30% or more during the same period.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Cross-Default Provisions
elements.label: text

elements.text: The occurrence of any of the following (to the extent not cured within any applicable cure period in
elements.label: text

elements.text: the applicable agreement):
elements.label: text

elements.text: • a breach by the seller/servicer on a credit or funding facility, including warehouse or servicing
elements.label: text

elements.text: advance lines of credit;
elements.label: text

elements.text: • a breach by any seller/servicer's affiliate or related entity in any of its obligations with Fannie Mae,
elements.label: text

elements.text: including parental guarantees;
elements.label: text

elements.text: • a breach of any agreements with any other creditors where such breach involves an amount that
elements.label: text

elements.text: exceeds 3% of the seller/servicer's Lender Adjusted Net Worth;
elements.label: text

elements.text: • a breach of, or an impairment of any rights contained in any agreement that is material to the
elements.label: text

elements.text: seller/servicer's origination of loans eligible for sale to Fannie Mae, servicing of Fannie Mae loans, the
elements.label: text

elements.text: financial or business condition or operations of the seller/servicer, or the seller/servicer's ability to
elements.label: text

elements.text: comply with the Lender Contract;
elements.label: text

elements.text: • the occurrence of any change event, or circumstance which has or could reasonably be expected to
elements.label: text

elements.text: have a material adverse effect on the seller/servicer's origination of loans, servicing of Fannie Mae
elements.label: text

elements.text: loans, on the financial or business condition or operations of the seller/servicer, or the ability to
elements.label: text

elements.text: comply with the Lender Contract.
elements.label: text

elements.text: An impairment of rights is any event that may restrict the ability of the seller or servicer to conduct
elements.label: text

elements.text: its business, continue to sell loans, or service any loan assets in the regular course of its business.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The seller/servicer must provide Fannie Mae with written notification in the form of an updated
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lender Record Information
elements.label: text

elements.text: ( Form 582) submission and email notification to the Changes in Lender
elements.label: text

elements.text: Organization mailbox (see E-1-02, List of Contacts) within five business days of the occurrence of any
elements.label: text

elements.text: of the foregoing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Recourse Obligation
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae may permit a seller/servicer to take on credit recourse obligations, provided the seller/servicer meets certain requirements. Fannie Mae will assess the financial strength of the seller/servicer to determine whether the seller/servicer can take on credit recourse obligations and, if permitted, whether the seller/servicer must post collateral or provide other forms of risk reduction measures to secure the additional obligations.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Business Continuity Management and Disaster Recovery
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Fannie Mae requires seller/servicer to have a Business Continuity Plan. This plan must include Business Continuity Procedures and Disaster Recovery Procedures, as defined in the Supplement. It must also identify business processes critical to the continuation of services and operations including those necessary to comply with obligations the seller/servicer has to Fannie Mae under the Lender Contract. The seller/servicer's Business Continuity Plan, Business Continuity Procedures and Disaster Recovery Procedures must meet the requirements of the Supplement .
elements.label: text

elements.text: Additionally, the seller/servicer must ensure it and any subservicers, third-party originators, outsourcing firms, and third-party vendors used by the seller/servicer implement and maintain disaster recovery and business continuity procedures. These procedures must ensure the ability to regain critical business operations if there is a disruption or disaster. This includes back-up systems, procedures and processes in the event of the expiration or termination of any contract that is material for seller/servicer's servicing of Fannie Mae loans, origination of loans for sale to Fannie Mae, or ability to comply with the Lender Contract. The lender must provide a copy of its Business Continuity Plan and Business Continuity Procedures to Fannie Mae upon Fannie Mae's written request.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Audit and Management Control Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The seller/servicer must have internal audit and management control procedures to evaluate and monitor the overall quality of its loan production and servicing processes, as applicable. At a minimum:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The procedures must be independent of all key functions of the loan manufacturing process and the servicing processes that they review, so that such procedures provide an objective and unbiased evaluation that adds value and improves the seller/servicer's operations.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The seller/servicer's lines of reporting must reflect the independence of the audit process at all levels, resulting in activities that are conducted in an unbiased manner and without quality compromises resulting from internal influences or conflicts of interest.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The audit function must not share any reporting lines with the functional areas that it reviews.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The audit function must report directly to the seller/servicer's senior management and/or board of directors. Exceptions are permitted in situations in which the size of the seller/servicer's organization is insufficient to support adequate resources to allow for separation of these functions. In those situations, the seller/servicer's audit plan must include the rationale for the lack of separation as well as the controls that have been established to mitigate the risks associated with the lack of separation of these functions.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The procedures must be consultative, so that they help the seller/servicer accomplish its objectives by bringing a systematic, disciplined approach to evaluating and improving the effectiveness of risk management, control, and governance processes.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Written Policies and Procedures
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The seller should have effective and fully documented written policies and/or procedures that ensure that its staff, and any outsourcing and third-party vendors used by the seller, consistently comply with Fannie Mae's requirements. This may include providing training to its staff and reviewing staffing needs on an ongoing basis.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Management of Vendors and Other Third-party Service Providers
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders must have written procedures for the approval and management of vendors and other third-party service providers. The procedures must comply with the business continuity requirements in the Supplement if the seller/servicer or any subservicer contracts with a vendor or third-party service provider for any critical servicing functions or ability to comply with the Lender Contract.
elements.label: text

elements.text: This topic contains information on financial statements and reports, including:

elements.text: Financial Reporting Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The seller/servicer must demonstrate its financial adequacy to Fannie Mae. To accomplish this, Fannie Mae requires the seller/servicer to submit financial statements and other information described in this topic. A seller/servicer's failure to timely provide the required information may result in Fannie Mae declaring a breach of the Lender Contract.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A seller/servicer must submit their annual Audited Financial Statement (AFS) within 90 days after the end of the seller/servicer's fiscal year.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The requirements for the AFS are described in the following table.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The seller/servicer's AFS must...
elements.label: text

elements.text: be prepared under Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP).
elements.label: text

elements.text: include the opinion of an independent public accountant.
elements.label: text

elements.text: be comparative with the previous year's reports.
elements.label: text

elements.text: include all of the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: • a balance sheet,
elements.label: text

elements.text: • an income statement,
elements.label: text

elements.text: • a statement of retained earnings,
elements.label: text

elements.text: • a statement of additional paid-in capital,
elements.label: text

elements.text: • a statement of changes in financial position, and
elements.label: text

elements.text: • all related notes.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the seller/servicer's financial statement is consolidated with those of its parent or holding company, they must contain sufficient detail to enable Fannie Mae to review the seller/servicer's financial data separately from that of the other companies.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The AFS must be sent electronically to Fannie Mae's Lender Eligibility and Compliance Unit (see E-1-02, List of Contacts). For submission instructions, see the Audited Financial Statement (AFS) page.

elements.text: Lender Record Information Form
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The Lender Record Information ( Form 582) provides information needed to verify that the seller/servicer continues to meet Fannie Mae's basic eligibility requirements as well as certifications regarding compliance with our requirements. The seller/servicer must update its Form 582 electronically no later than 90 days after the end of the seller/servicer's fiscal year. If there are changes to the lender's record information, the seller/servicer must update Form 582 and provide Fannie Mae with written notification in the form of an email to the Changes in Lender Organization mailbox (see E-1-02, List of Contacts) within five business days of the occurrence of any of the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: any actions pending, starting, or to the seller/servicer's knowledge, threatened against or involving the seller/servicer that could reasonably be expected to have a material adverse effect on the seller/servicer's ability to comply with provisions of the Lender Contract, its financial status, servicing operations, or mortgage operations;

elements.text: a breach of certain agreements, in accordance with A4-1-01, Maintaining Seller/Servicer Eligibility;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: any material and adverse change in the circumstances and qualifications that were in place for Fannie

elements.text: A4-1-03, Report of Changes in the Seller/Servicer's Organization (05/01/2024)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on the reporting of changes in the seller/servicer's organization.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Report of Changes in the Seller/Servicer's Organization
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Report of Changes in the Seller/Servicer's Organization
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The seller/servicer must send us 60 days' advance written notice of any proposed major change in its organization to allow us adequate time to provide our prior written approval or notice of non-objection or objection, where required. The written notice must include copies of any filings with, or approvals from, the seller/servicer's state or other regulatory authority. The seller/servicer is also required to provide immediate written notice to us if a regulatory agency assumes a participatory role in the management of the seller/servicer's operations.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A seller/servicer's failure to provide adequate written notice of or obtain prior written approval or notice of nonobjection (where required) for such contemplated changes is a breach of the Lender Contract.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The seller/servicer must contact its customer account team for additional guidance or may email the notice to the Changes in Lender Organization mailbox (see E-1-02, List of Contacts).
elements.label: text

elements.text: In addition to advance written notice, major changes of the following types require Fannie Mae's prior written approval of or notice of non-objection before the change is made.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Type of Change
elements.label: text

elements.text: Any mergers, consolidations, or reorganizations
elements.label: text

elements.text: The sale of all or substantially all of the seller/servicer's assets or the purchase of all or
elements.label: text

elements.text: substantially all of the assets of another Fannie Mae-approved seller or servicer
elements.label: text

elements.text: Any substantial change in ownership (including a new owner or partner with a 5% or more
elements.label: text

elements.text: interest), regardless of whether it is by direct or indirect means (indirect means include any
elements.label: text

elements.text: change in the ownership of the seller/servicer's parent, any owner of the parent, or any other
elements.label: text

elements.text: beneficial owner of the seller/servicer that does not own a direct interest in the seller/servicer)
elements.label: text

elements.text: A change in an organization's legal structure or charter
elements.label: text

elements.text: Other major changes require advance written notification in the form of an email to the Changes in Lender Organization mailbox (see E-1-02, List of Contacts), but do not require Fannie Mae's prior written approval or notice of non-objection. Examples of those types of changes are described below.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Examples
elements.label: text

elements.text: The change of any senior management personnel (including principal officers or decision
elements.label: text

elements.text: Submitting Irrevocable Limited Power of Attorney (Form 520) to Fannie Mae
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Seller/servicers must complete and deliver five executed original Form 520 to Fannie Mae at the address indicated on the instructions to the form.  See below for an exception to this requirement.
elements.label: text

elements.text: All signatures on the  Form 520 must be provided using traditional in-person ink-signed methods. Irrespective of the law of the place of execution, the notary may not use the remote in-person method of signatory authentication and signature witnessing or eNotarization, including remote online notarization, to satisfy the notarization of the Form 520.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The Form 520 does not modify the duties and obligations of the parties under the Lender Contract. The Form 520 is a free-standing remedial instrument intended to preserve and protect Fannie Mae's interest in loans serviced by the seller/servicer. In the event of exigent circumstances, Fannie Mae will use the power of attorney at its sole discretion (for example, when for whatever reason a seller/servicer is unable or unwilling to execute requested assignments of mortgages on a timely basis).
elements.label: text

elements.text: In lieu of the Form 520, a seller/servicer that is a housing finance agency, as defined in 24 C.F.R. §266.5, may submit a letter confirming that it meets such definition.  After Fannie Mae has reviewed and verified compliance with the definition, the seller/servicer will be exempt from this requirement.

elements.text: Documenting the Loan Application
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A loan application must be documented on the Uniform Residential Loan Application (Form 1003). A complete, signed, and dated version of the final Form 1003 must always be included in the loan file. The final Form 1003 must reflect the income, assets, debts, and final loan terms used in the underwriting process. (See B3-2-10, Accuracy of DU Data, DU Tolerances, and Errors in the Credit Report for additional information about ensuring DU data accuracy and tolerances.)
elements.label: text

elements.text: If either the note or the security instrument and the final Form 1003 will be executed pursuant to a power of attorney in accordance with this Guide, then the initial Form 1003 must be personally signed by the borrower (except as provided below) and included in the loan file. See B8-2-03, Signature Requirements for Security Instruments, B8-3-03, Signature Requirements for Notes, and B8-5-05, Requirements for Use of a Power of Attorney, for additional information. However, a power of attorney may be used to execute both the initial and final Form 1003 in any of the following circumstances:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: a borrower is on military service with the United States armed forces serving outside the United States or deployed aboard a United States vessel, as long as the power of attorney
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: expressly states an intention to secure a loan on a specific property, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: complies with the requirements under the VA Lender's Handbook relating to powers of attorney for VA-insured mortgage loans,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the attorney-in-fact or agent signs the security instrument in their personal capacity with regard to their individual ownership interest in the mortgaged property; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the lender determines such use is required by applicable law.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note : The terms 'loan application' and 'Form 1003' generally mean the same thing in this Guide. The term 'online loan application' refers to the DU user interface (or the lender's loan origination system) where data is collected from the Form 1003, in addition to other loan data used in underwriting.

elements.text: Blanket Authorization Form
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender obtains the borrower's signature on the following Blanket Authorization form to obtain the documentation needed to evaluate the borrower's creditworthiness.
elements.label: text

elements.text: This borrower-signed document gives the lender blanket authorization to request the information needed to document the borrower's creditworthiness. I hereby authorize ___________________________ (the 'lender') to verify my past and present employment earnings records, bank accounts, stock holdings, and any other asset balances that are needed to process my mortgage loan application. I further authorize ______________________ (the 'lender') to order a consumer credit report and verify other credit information, including past and present mortgage and landlord references. It is understood that a photocopy of this form also will serve as authorization. The information the lender obtains is only to be used in the processing of my application for a mortgage loan.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender attaches a copy of the Blanket Authorization form to each Form 1005 or Form 1006 sent to a verifying institution. The information must be requested directly from the institution. The completed form(s) must be signed and dated, and must be sent directly from the verifying institution.

elements.text: B1-1-03, Allowable Age of Credit Documents and Federal Income Tax Returns (04/02/2025)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on the allowable age of credit documents and federal income tax returns.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Allowable Age of Credit Documents
elements.label: list_item

elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Allowable Age of Credit Documents
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Credit documents include credit reports and employment, income, and asset documentation. For all mortgage loans (existing and new construction), the credit documents must be no more than four months old on the note date. When consecutive credit documents are in the loan file, the most recent document is used to determine whether it meets the age requirement. For example, when two consecutive monthly bank statements are used to verify a depository asset, the date of the most recent statement must be no more than four months old on the note date. If the credit documents are older than allowed, the lender must update them. For age requirements related to appraisals, see B4-1.2-04, Appraisal Age and Use Requirements. Also see B2-3-05, Properties Affected by a Disaster for exceptions to the allowable age of credit documents for loans impacted by a natural disaster.

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes mortgages secured by properties that are principal residences, second homes, or investment properties. For the maximum allowable LTV/CLTV/HCLTV ratios and credit score requirements for each occupancy type, see the Eligibility Matrix.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Principal Residence Properties
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A principal residence is a property that the borrower occupies as their primary residence. The following table describes conditions under which Fannie Mae considers a residence to be a principal residence even though the borrower will not be occupying the property.

elements.text: Investment Properties
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: An investment property is owned but not occupied by the borrower. An LLPA applies to all mortgage loans secured by an investment property. These LLPAs are in addition to any other price adjustments that are otherwise applicable to the particular transaction. See the Loan-Level Price Adjustment (LLPA) Matrix.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Loans secured by an investment property must be underwritten in DU and receive an Approve/Eligible recommendation, with the exception of high LTV refinance loans that are required to be underwritten in accordance with the Alternative Qualification Path (see B5-7-03, High LTV Refinance Alternative Qualification Path).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Defining Occupancy for a Group Home
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Eligibility and pricing for group homes will be the same as currently provided under the terms and conditions established for principal residence, second home, or investment properties depending on the particular occupancy status of the borrower(s).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Investment properties that are or will be leased to business entities for use as a group home are eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae (provided all borrowers are individuals).
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the lender identifies rental income from the property, the loan is eligible for delivery as a second home as long as the income is not used for qualifying purposes, and all other requirements for second homes are met (including the occupancy requirement above). 1

elements.text: Calculation of the LTV Ratio
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The maximum allowable LTV ratio for a first mortgage is based on a number of factors including, the representative credit score, the type of mortgage product, the number of dwelling units, and the occupancy status of the property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The following table describes the requirements for calculating LTV ratios for a first mortgage transaction. The result of these calculations must be truncated (shortened) to two decimal places, then rounded up to the nearest whole percent. For example:
elements.label: text

elements.text: 94.01% will be delivered as 95%, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: 80.001% will be delivered as 80%.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The rounding rules noted above also apply to the CLTV and HCLTV ratio calculations. Lenders' systems must contain rounding methodology that results in the same or a higher LTV ratio.

elements.text: Sales Price and Appraised Value Used by DU
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: DU uses information in the loan application to determine the sales price and appraised value it uses to calculate the LTV, CLTV, and HCLTV ratios.
elements.label: text

elements.text: DU uses the amounts entered in the following data fields in the online loan application:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Sales price = Line A + Line B + Line C (in Section L4)
elements.label: text

elements.text: Line A = Sales Contract Price (the sales price for purchase transactions, or the cost of construction for construction transactions).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Line B = Improvements, Renovations,  and Repairs (the cost of these items for HomeStyle Renovation transactions).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Line C = Land Value (For certain construction or manufactured home transactions the cost or value of the land if the borrower acquired the lot separately).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Appraised value = Appraised Property Value (in Section 4a)
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : If the Appraised Property Value is not present, DU will use the Estimated Property Value. The Appraised Property Value must be entered (when available) and the loan casefile must be resubmitted.
elements.label: text

elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on CLTV ratios, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Calculation of the CLTV Ratio
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Loan-Level Price Adjustments

elements.text: Calculation of the CLTV Ratio
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For first mortgage loans that are subject to subordinate financing, the lender must calculate the LTV ratio and the CLTV ratio. For first mortgage loans that are subject to a HELOC, see B2-1.2-03, Home Equity Combined Loan-to-Value (HCLTV) Ratios. For all other subordinate liens, see B2-1.2-04, Subordinate Financing for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The CLTV ratio is determined by dividing the sum of the items listed below by the lesser of the sales price or the appraised value of the property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: the original loan amount of the first mortgage,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the drawn portion (outstanding principal balance) of a HELOC, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the unpaid principal balance of all closed-end subordinate financing. (With a closed-end loan, a borrower draws down all funds on day one and may not make any payment plan changes or access any paid-down
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: principal once the loan is closed.)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Note: For each subordinate liability, in order for the lender to accurately calculate the CLTV ratio for eligibility and underwriting purposes, the lender must determine the drawn portion of all HELOCs, if applicable, and the unpaid principal balance for all closed-end subordinate financing. If any subordinate financing is not shown on a credit report, the lender must obtain documentation from the borrower or creditor.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the borrower discloses, or the lender discovers, new (or increased) subordinate financing after the underwriting decision has been made, up to and concurrent with closing, the lender must re-underwrite the mortgage loan. (See B3-6-02, Debt-to-Income Ratios, for additional information.)
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: The CLTV ratio calculation may differ for certain mortgage loans. For details on these differences, see  B2-1.3-05, Payoff of Installment Land Contract Requirements; B5-2-03, Manufactured Housing Underwriting Requirements; B5-3.1-02, Conversion of Construction-to-Permanent Financing: Single-Closing Transactions; B5-3.2-03, HomeStyle Renovation Mortgages: Collateral Considerations; B5-3.3-01, HomeStyle Refresh for Improvements on Existing Properties; B5-5.1-02, Community Seconds Loan Eligibility; and  B5-5.2-02, Loans with Resale Restrictions: Eligibility, Collateral and Delivery Requirements; and B5-5.3-03, Shared Equity Transactions: Eligibility, Underwriting and Collateral Requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Refer to the  Eligibility Matrix  for allowable CLTV ratios.
elements.label: text

elements.label: section_header

elements.text: An LLPA applies to certain mortgages with subordinate financing. These LLPAs are in addition to any other price adjustments that are otherwise applicable to the particular transaction. See the  Loan-Level Price Adjustment (LLPA) Matrix.

elements.text: B2-1.2-03, Home Equity Combined Loan-to-Value (HCLTV) Ratios (02/23/2016)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on HCLTV ratios, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Calculation of the HCLTV Ratio
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Permanently Modified HELOCs
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Calculation of the HCLTV Ratio
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For first mortgages that have subordinate financing under a HELOC, the lender must calculate the HCLTV ratio. This is determined by dividing the sum of the items listed below by the lesser of the sales price or appraised value of the property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: the original loan amount of the first mortgage,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the full amount of any HELOCs (whether or not funds have been drawn), and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the unpaid principal balance (UPB) of all closed-end subordinate financing.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note : For each subordinate liability, in order for the lender to accurately calculate the HCLTV ratio for eligibility and underwriting purposes, the lender must determine the maximum credit line for all HELOCs, if applicable, and the unpaid principal balance for all closed-end subordinate financing. If any subordinate financing is not shown on a credit report, the lender must obtain documentation from the borrower or creditor.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the borrower discloses, or the lender discovers, new (or increased) subordinate financing after the underwriting decision has been made, up to and concurrent with closing, the lender must reunderwrite the mortgage loan. (See B3-6-02, Debt-to-Income Ratios, for additional information.)
elements.label: text

elements.text: Permanently Modified HELOCs
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If the lender determines the HELOC has been permanently modified and the outstanding UPB is less than the permanently modified HELOC, the lender must use the modified HELOC amount in calculating the HCLTV ratio for eligibility purposes and for delivery. The lender must obtain appropriate documentation that the HELOC has been permanently modified and include this documentation in the loan file.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the outstanding UPB is greater than the permanently modified HELOC, the lender must use the outstanding UPB to calculate the HCLTV ratio for eligibility purposes and for delivery. As noted above, the lender must obtain appropriate documentation and include that documentation in the loan file.
elements.label: text

elements.text: In no case may the CLTV ratio exceed the HCLTV ratio.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : The HCLTV ratio calculation may differ for certain mortgage loans. For details on these differences, see B2-1.3-05, Payoff of Installment Land Contract Requirements; B5-2-03, Manufactured Housing Underwriting Requirements; B5-3.1-02, Conversion of Construction-to-Permanent Financing: Single-Closing Transactions; B5-3.3-01, HomeStyle Refresh for Improvements on Existing Properties; B5-3.2-03, HomeStyle Renovation Mortgages: Collateral Considerations; and B5-5.1-02, Community Seconds Loan Eligibility.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : Refer to the Eligibility Matrix for maximum allowable HCLTV ratios.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Subordinate Financing Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes first-lien loans where the secured property is subject to subordinate financing. (See B5-7-01, High LTV Refinance Loan and Borrower Eligibility for exceptions to this policy.) Subordinate liens must be
elements.label: text

elements.text: evidenced by a promissory note;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: reflected in a recorded mortgage, deed of trust, or other security instrument; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: clearly subordinate to Fannie Mae's first mortgage loan or co-op share loan.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lenders must disclose the existence of subordinate financing and the subordinate financing repayment terms to Fannie Mae, the appraiser, and the mortgage insurer.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: Unless it qualifies as a Community Seconds loan, an agreement under which a borrower is obligated to pay a third-party (other than a co-owner of the subject property) a share in any appreciation in the value of the subject property is not permitted.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender must consider all subordinate liens secured by the subject property, regardless of the obligated party, when calculating CLTV and HCLTV ratios. This includes business loans, such as those provided by the Small Business Administration.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Except as described in Acceptable Subordinate Financing below or under Section B5-5.1, Community Seconds, no other type of recorded instrument documenting or securing the borrower's obligation to pay an amount in connection with funds advanced to the borrower in relation to the first mortgage is permitted, unless those funds have been advanced to the borrower by a co-owner of the subject property. Regardless of whether it qualifies as financing, eligible subordinated or unsecured PACE structures are permitted in accordance with B5-3.4-01, Property Assessed Clean Energy Loans.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For more information on subordinate financing originated in connection with the Section 502 Leveraged (Blended) Loan Program, see B6-1-05, Eligible RD-Guaranteed Mortgages.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Acceptable Subordinate Financing
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: The table below provides the requirements for acceptable subordinate financing, other than an eligible Community Seconds loan.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Acceptable Subordinate Financing
elements.label: text

elements.text: Variable payment mortgages that comply with the details below.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Mortgages with regular payments that cover at least the interest due so that negative amortization
elements.label: text

elements.text: does not occur.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Mortgages with deferred payments in connection with employer subordinate financing (see below).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Mortgage terms that require interest at a market rate.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the interest rate for financing provided by the property seller is more than 2% below current standard rates for second mortgages, the subordinate financing must be considered a sales concession and the subordinate financing amount must be deducted from the sales price.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Unacceptable Subordinate Financing Terms
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The table below describes examples of unacceptable subordinate financing terms on financing that does not qualify as an eligible Community Seconds mortgage.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Unacceptable Subordinate Financing Terms
elements.label: text

elements.text: Mortgages with negative amortization (with the exception of employer subordinate financing
elements.label: text

elements.text: that has deferred payments).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Subordinate financing that does not fully amortize under a level monthly payment plan where
elements.label: text

elements.text: the maturity or balloon payment date is less than five years after the note date of the new
elements.label: text

elements.text: first mortgage (with the exception of employer subordinate financing that has deferred
elements.label: text

elements.text: payments).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note
elements.label: text

elements.text: : Fannie Mae will accept these subordinate financing terms when the amount of the
elements.label: text

elements.text: subordinate debt is minimal relative to the borrower's financial assets and/or credit profile.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For additional information about applicable subordinate financing policies, see the following topics:
elements.label: text

elements.text: B5-5.1-02, Community Seconds Loan Eligibility,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B4-2.3-04, Loan Eligibility for Co-op Share Loans, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B5-7-01, High LTV Refinance Loan and Borrower Eligibility.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Eligible Variable Payment Terms for Subordinate Financing
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae permits variable payments for subordinate financing that does not qualify as an eligible Community Seconds loan if the following provisions are met:
elements.label: text

elements.text: With the exception of HELOCs, when the repayment terms provide for a variable interest rate, the monthly payment must remain constant for each 12-month period over the term of the subordinate mortgage. (For HELOCs, the monthly payment does not have to remain constant.)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The monthly payments for all subordinate liens must cover at least the interest due so that negative amortization does not occur (with the exception of employer subordinate financing that has deferred payments).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Eligible Repayment Terms for Employer Subordinate Financing
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If the subordinate financing is from the borrower's employer, it does not have to require regular payments of either principal and interest or interest only. Employer subordinate financing may be structured in any of the following ways:
elements.label: text

elements.text: fully amortizing level monthly payments,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: deferred payments for some period before changing to fully amortizing level payments,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: deferred payments over the entire term, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: forgiveness of the debt over time.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The financing terms may provide for the employer to require full repayment of the debt if the borrower's employment is terminated (either voluntarily or involuntarily) before the maturity date of the subordinate financing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Refer to B3-4.3-08, Employer Assistance, for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Resubordination Requirements for Refinance Transactions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If subordinate financing is left in place in connection with a first mortgage refinance transaction, Fannie Mae requires execution and recordation of a resubordination agreement.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If state law permits subordinate financing to remain in the same subordinate lien position established with the prior first mortgage that is being refinanced, Fannie Mae does not require resubordination. The subordinate lien must satisfy any specified criteria of the applicable statutes.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : Title insurance against the fact that a former subordinate lien is not properly resubordinated to the refinance loan does not release lenders from compliance with these resubordination requirements, or from Fannie Mae's requirement that the property is free and clear of all encumbrances and liens having priority over Fannie Mae's loan.

elements.text: General Purchase Transaction Eligibility Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A purchase money transaction is one in which the proceeds are used to finance the acquisition of a property or to finance the acquisition and rehabilitation of a property. The table below provides the general requirements for purchase money mortgage transactions. Certain mortgage loans and products may have different eligibility requirements for purchase mortgage transactions. If applicable, the differences will be stated in the specific mortgage loan or product topic section.
elements.label: text

elements.text: General Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The minimum borrower contribution requirements for the selected mortgage loan type must
elements.label: text

elements.text: be met.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Proceeds from the transaction must be used to
elements.label: text

elements.text: • finance the acquisition of the subject property,
elements.label: text

elements.text: • finance the acquisition and rehabilitation of the subject property,
elements.label: text

elements.text: • convert an interim construction loan or term note into permanent financing, or
elements.label: text

elements.text: • pay off the outstanding balance on the installment land contract or contract for deed.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: General Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Proceeds from the transaction may not be used to give the borrower cash back other than the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: an amount representing reimbursement for the borrower's overpayment of fees and charges, including refunds that may be required in accordance with certain federal laws or regulations. The settlement statement must clearly indicate the refund, and the loan file must include documentation to support the amount and reason for the refund; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a legitimate prorated real estate tax credit in locales where real estate taxes are paid in arrears.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note : If the borrower receives cash back for a permissible purpose as listed above, the lender must confirm the minimum borrower contribution requirements associated with the selected mortgage product, if any, have been met. Reimbursements or refunds permitted above may also be applied as a principal curtailment in accordance with B2-1.5-05, Principal Curtailments. A prorated real estate tax credit is not an interested party contribution, and it cannot be considered when determining if the borrower has sufficient assets for the transaction.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Exception : When the Closing Disclosure indicates an escrow account has been established and includes the portion of real estate taxes owed by the property seller for the period they owned the property, a prorated real estate tax credit from the property seller may offset all or a portion of the funds needed for the escrow account.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Non-Arm's Length Transactions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Non-arm's length transactions are purchase transactions in which there is a relationship or business affiliation between the seller and the buyer of the property. Fannie Mae allows non-arm's length transactions for the purchase of existing properties unless specifically forbidden for the particular scenario, such as delayed financing. For the purchase of newly constructed properties, if the borrower has a relationship or business affiliation (any ownership interest, or employment) with the builder, developer, or seller of the property, Fannie Mae will only purchase mortgage loans secured by a principal residence. Fannie Mae will not purchase mortgage loans on newly constructed homes secured by a second home or investment property if the borrower has a relationship or business affiliation with the builder, developer, or seller of the property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Purchase of Preforeclosure or Short Sale Properties - Allowable Fees, Assessments, and Payments
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Borrowers may pay additional fees, assessments, or payments in connection with acquiring a property that is a preforeclosure or short sale that are typically the responsibility of the seller or another party. Examples of additional fees, assessments, or payments include, but are not limited to, the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: short sale processing fees (also referred to as short sale negotiation fees, buyer discount fees, short sale
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: buyer fees);
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Note : This fee does not represent a common and customary charge and therefore must be treated as a sales concession if any portion is reimbursed by an interested party to the transaction.
elements.label: text

elements.text: payment to a subordinate lienholder; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: payment of delinquent taxes or delinquent HOA assessments.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The following requirements apply:
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The borrower (buyer) must be provided with written details of the additional fees, assessments, or payments and the additional necessary funds to complete the transaction must be documented.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The servicer that is agreeing to the preforeclosure or short sale must be provided with written details of the fees, assessments, or payments and has the option of renegotiating the payoff amount to release its lien.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: All parties (buyer, seller, and servicer) must provide their written agreement of the final details of the transaction which must include the additional fees, assessments, or payments. This can be accomplished by using the Request for Approval of Short Sale or Alternative Request for the Approval of Short Sale forms published by the U.S. Treasury or any alternative form or addendum.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The settlement statement must include all fees, assessments, and payments included in the transaction.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B2-1.3-02, Limited Cash-Out Refinance Transactions (10/08/2025)

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on limited cash-out refinance transactions, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Eligibility Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.label: list_item

elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Eligibility Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Limited cash-out refinance transactions must meet the following requirements:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The transaction is being used to obtain a new first mortgage secured by the same property to
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: pay off an existing first mortgage (including an existing HELOC in first-lien position);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: pay off an existing construction loan and documented construction cost overruns that were incurred outside of the interim construction financing for two-closing construction-to-permanent loans. (These construction cost overruns must be paid directly to the builder at closing.);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: pay for construction costs to build the home for single-closing construction-to-permanent loans, which may include paying off an existing lot lien; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: pay off an installment land contract that was executed more than 12 months before the date of the loan application.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: At least one borrower on the new loan must be an owner (on title) of the subject property at the time of the initial application. Exceptions are allowed if the lender documents that
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the borrower acquired the property through an inheritance or was legally awarded the property (such as through a divorce, separation, or dissolution of a domestic partnership); or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the property was previously owned by an inter vivos revocable trust and the borrower is the primary beneficiary of the trust;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the borrower is currently financially obligated on the loan being paid off but not on the title. This includes loans where the property is currently owned by a limited liability corporation (LLC) that is majority owned or controlled by the borrower(s). Ownership must be transferred into the name of the individual borrower(s). See B2-2-01, General Borrower Eligibility Requirements  for additional requirements; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the borrower is paying off an installment land contract that was executed more than 12 months before the date of the loan application.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Only subordinate liens used to purchase the property may be paid off and included in the new mortgage. Exceptions are allowed for paying off a Property Assessed Clean Energy (PACE) loan or other debt (secured or unsecured) that was used solely for energy-related improvements. See B5-3.3-01, HomeStyle Refresh for Improvements on Existing Properties, for additional information.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the subject property was previously listed for sale, it must have been taken off the market on or before the disbursement date of the new loan.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Additional Requirements for Limited Cash-Out Refinance Transactions with LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV Ratios of 95.01 - 97%
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If the LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV ratio exceeds 95% for a limited cash-out transaction, the following requirements also apply.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Criteria
elements.label: text

elements.text: Requirements
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender must inform DU that the existing loan is
elements.label: text

elements.text: owned (or securitized) by Fannie Mae using the
elements.label: text

elements.text: Owner of Existing Mortgage field in the online loan
elements.label: text

elements.text: application before submitting the loan to DU.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The DU message indicating the borrower's existing
elements.label: text

elements.text: loan was identified as a Fannie Mae loan may be
elements.label: text

elements.text: relied upon as confirmation the loan is owned by
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When DU is not able to identify the borrower's
elements.label: text

elements.text: existing loan is owned (or securitized) by Fannie Mae,
elements.label: text

elements.text: the lender must provide documentation.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Documentation may come from
elements.label: text

elements.text: • the lender's servicing system,
elements.label: text

elements.text: • the current servicer (if the lender is not the
elements.label: text

elements.text: servicer),
elements.label: text

elements.text: • Fannie Mae's Loan Lookup tool, or
elements.label: text

elements.text: • any other source as confirmed by the lender.

elements.label: text

elements.text: : This requirement does not apply if the CLTV
elements.label: text

elements.text: exceeds 95% only due to a Community Seconds loan.
elements.label: text

elements.text: LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV Ratio
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note
elements.label: text

elements.text: : The CLTV ratio can be up to 105% if the
elements.label: text

elements.text: subordinate lien is a Community Seconds loan.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Loan Type
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fixed-rate loans with terms up to 30 years.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note

elements.text: : High-balance and ARM loans are not permitted.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Criteria
elements.label: text

elements.text: Requirements
elements.label: text

elements.text: Property and Occupancy
elements.label: text

elements.text: One-unit principal residence. All borrowers must
elements.label: text

elements.text: occupy the property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Manufactured housing is not permitted, unless the
elements.label: text

elements.text: property meets the MH Advantage requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Underwriting Method
elements.label: text

elements.text: DU only
elements.label: text

elements.text: Other
elements.label: text

elements.text: All other standard limited cash-out refinance policies
elements.label: text

elements.text: apply.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: The above requirements do not apply to HomeReady or high LTV refinance loans. For additional information, see B5-6-01, HomeReady Mortgage Loan and Borrower Eligibility or B5-7-01, High LTV Refinance Loan and Borrower Eligibility accordingly.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Ineligible Transactions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When the following conditions exist, the transaction is ineligible as a limited cash-out refinance and must be treated as a cash-out refinance:
elements.label: text

elements.text: no outstanding first lien on the subject property (except for single-closing construction-to-permanent transactions, which are eligible as a limited cash-out out refinance even though there is not an outstanding lien on the subject property);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the proceeds are used to pay off a subordinate lien that was not used to purchase the property (other than the exceptions for paying off PACE loans and other debt used for energy-related improvements, described above);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the borrower finances the payment of real estate taxes that are more than 60 days delinquent for the subject property in the loan amount; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a short-term refinance mortgage loan that combines a first mortgage and a non-purchase-money subordinate mortgage into a new first mortgage or any refinance of that loan within six months.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: See also B2-1.3-04, Prohibited Refinancing Practices.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Acceptable Uses
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The following are acceptable in conjunction with a limited cash-out refinance transaction:
elements.label: text

elements.text: modifying the interest rate and/or term for existing mortgages;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: paying off the existing first mortgage (which may include additional amounts required to pay off the loan, such as prepayment penalties, a deferred balance resulting from completion of a prior loss mitigation solution, and late fees);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: paying for construction costs to build a home for a single-closing construction-to-permanent transaction,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: which may include paying off an existing lot lien;
elements.label: text

elements.text: paying off the construction loan and documented construction cost overruns for a two-closing construction-to-permanent loan;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: paying off the installment land contract that was executed more than 12 months before the date of the loan application;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: financing the payment of closing costs, points, and prepaid items. With the exception of real estate taxes that are more than 60 days delinquent the borrower can include real estate taxes in the new loan amount provided
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the real estate taxes must be paid in full through the transaction, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: payment for the taxes must be disbursed to the taxing authority through the closing transaction, with no funds used for the taxes disbursed to the borrower;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: providing cash back to the borrower (or any other party) in an amount that, in aggregate, does not exceed the greater of 1% of the new refinance loan amount or $2,000;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: buying out a co-owner pursuant to an agreement;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: paying off a subordinate mortgage lien (including prepayment penalties) used to purchase the subject property. (When the subordinate loan is a Community Seconds, payoff may include any required payment of the share of appreciation due to the Community Seconds provider under the terms of the shared appreciation agreement.)The lender must document that the entire amount of the subordinate financing was used to acquire the property; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: paying off the unpaid principal balance of PACE loans and other debt used for energy-related improvements, described above.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Cash Back
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: As noted above, the lender may provide a small amount of cash back in a limited cash-out refinance transaction. The lender may also refund the borrower for the overpayment of fees and charges due to federal or state laws or regulations. Refunds such as these are not included in the maximum cash back limitation, provided that
elements.label: text

elements.text: the settlement statement clearly identifies the refund, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the loan file includes documentation to support the amount and reason for the refund.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: This applies to standard limited cash-out refinance transactions. For high LTV refinance transactions, see B5-7-01, High LTV Refinance Loan and Borrower Eligibility.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : These refunds may also be applied as a principal balance curtailment in accordance with B2-1.5-05, Principal Curtailments.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Documentation Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: To treat a transaction as a limited cash-out refinance transaction, the lender must document that all proceeds of the existing subordinate lien were used to fund part of the subject property purchase price or pay for permissible energy-related expenses. Written confirmation must be maintained in the mortgage file.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The following are acceptable forms of documentation:
elements.label: text

elements.text: a copy of the settlement statement for the purchase of the property;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a copy of the title policy from the purchase transaction that identifies the subordinate financing;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: other documentation from the purchase transaction that indicates that a subordinate lien was used to purchase the subject property; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: for energy-related expenses, copies of invoices or receipts to evidence funds were used for energy improvements. A copy of an energy report is required in many cases. See B5-3.3-01, HomeStyle Refresh for Improvements on Existing Properties, for additional information.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Existing Subordinate Liens That Will Not Be Paid Off
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When a new limited cash-out refinance transaction will not satisfy existing subordinate liens, the existing liens must be clearly subordinate to the new refinance mortgage. The refinance mortgage must meet Fannie Mae's eligibility criteria for mortgages that are subject to subordinate financing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: New Subordinate Financing
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When a borrower obtains new subordinate financing with the refinancing of a first mortgage loan, Fannie Mae treats the transaction as a limited cash-out refinance provided the first mortgage loan meets the eligibility criteria for a limited cash-out refinance transaction.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : It is acceptable for borrowers to obtain cash from the proceeds of the new subordinate mortgage.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Refinances to Buy Out An Owner's Interest
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A transaction that requires one owner to buy out the interest of another owner (for example, as a result of a divorce settlement or dissolution of a domestic partnership) is considered a limited cash-out refinance if the secured property was jointly owned for at least 12 months preceding the disbursement date of the new mortgage loan.
elements.label: text

elements.text: All parties must sign a written agreement that states the terms of the property transfer and the proposed disposition of the proceeds from the refinance transaction. Except in the case of recent inheritance of the subject property, documentation must be provided to indicate that the security property was jointly owned by all parties for at least 12 months preceding the disbursement date of the new mortgage loan.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Borrowers who acquire sole ownership of the property may not receive any of the proceeds from the refinancing. The party buying out the other party's interest must be able to qualify for the mortgage pursuant to Fannie Mae's underwriting guidelines.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Exceptions to Limited Cash-Out Refinance Requirements for High Loan-to-Value Refinance Loans
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: See Chapter B5-7: High Loan-to-Value Refinance Option , for modifications to the standard limited cash-out refinance requirements for high LTV loan transactions.
elements.label: text
